title: Kibbeh
subtitle: Lebanese bulgur wheat and lamb patties
excerpt: Fine bulgur wheat kneaded with minced lamb, onion, and allspice until smooth, then formed into oval patties and either fried until crunchy (kibbeh maqlieh) or baked. The outer shell is made from the kibbeh mixture itself, encasing a filling of coarser lamb mince cooked with onion, pine nuts, and warm spices.
sourceNotes: Kibbeh is considered the national dish of Lebanon and Syria and one of the oldest known meat preparations in the Levant. Anissa Helou's 'Lebanese Cuisine' (1994) documents multiple forms: fried, baked, raw (kibbeh nayyeh), and in soup. The combination of bulgur wheat with minced lamb is the defining Levantine technique. Fried kibbeh are the most widely eaten form outside Lebanon.

content.text: The kibbeh shell is made by processing or kneading very fine bulgur wheat with raw lamb mince, grated onion, allspice, and a little ice water. The mixture should be kneaded until it is completely smooth and cohesive, this is important, as a grainy or loose shell cracks during frying and the filling leaks out. A food processor handles this quickly; by hand it takes about 10 minutes of firm kneading.

Shaping kibbeh takes practice. Wet your hands, take a handful of the shell mixture, and form it into an oval. Push your thumb through the centre to create a cavity, then rotate the kibbeh on your finger, working the walls thin and even. Fill with a teaspoon of the cooked filling, then pinch the end closed. The walls should be about 5 mm thick. Chill for 30 minutes before frying to firm them up.

items.name: Bulgur wheat
items.prepNote: very fine (no. 1 grade), soaked in cold water for 10 minutes, drained and squeezed dry

content.text: Heat the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} over medium heat. Cook the chopped {{onion}} for 8 minutes until soft. Add the filling {{lamb-mince}} and cook, breaking it up, for 8 to 10 minutes until browned. Season with filling {{allspice}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, and salt. Stir in the {{pine-nuts}}. Cool completely.

Combine the drained {{bulgur-wheat}}, lean {{lamb-mince}}, grated {{onion}}, shell {{allspice}}, and salt. Knead or process until completely smooth, 8 to 10 minutes by hand or 2 minutes in a food processor. Add 1 to 2 tablespoons of ice water if the mixture is too stiff. With wet hands, take a golf-ball sized portion, form an oval, and hollow it out. Fill with 1 teaspoon of filling, pinch closed, and smooth the join. Place on a tray. Refrigerate for 30 minutes.

Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} in a deep saucepan to 180°C. Fry the kibbeh in batches of 3 to 4 for 4 to 5 minutes, turning once, until deep golden brown all over. Drain on kitchen paper. Serve hot with yoghurt and flatbread.

items.fix: Knead the shell mixture until completely uniform with no cracks or air pockets. Keep the walls at least 5 mm thick. The ends must be pinched tightly closed
items.cause: Shell too thin, air pockets in the shell, or shell not smooth enough
items.symptom: Kibbeh crack or burst during frying

items.fix: The ratio of bulgur to meat should be roughly equal. Over-processing in a food processor can tighten the mixture, knead by hand if this happens
items.cause: Too much bulgur relative to meat, or over-kneaded

items.fix: Pinch the ends firmly and roll between your palms to smooth the seal. The joint should be invisible. Chill before frying to firm everything up
Maintain 180°C. Frying too many at once drops the temperature and the kibbeh absorbs oil rather than frying crisp
items.cause: Oil not hot enough or too many in the pan at once

content.text: Kibbeh is the dish that Lebanese cooks point to as their greatest technical achievement: the ability to knead bulgur and raw lamb until they merge into a unified, workable shell is one of the fundamental skills of the Lebanese kitchen. Every family has its own recipe and its own way of shaping them. Kibbeh nayyeh (raw kibbeh), the shell mixture eaten raw like a steak tartare, is a separate preparation eaten as a delicacy. The fried version (kibbeh maqlieh) is the most widely eaten in the diaspora and in Lebanese restaurants worldwide. The baked version (kibbeh bil-sanieh) is similar to a layered tray bake with the kibbeh mixture as the base and top.
